topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly behind my eyes. I kept re-reading the performance review, the words blurring into an indecipherable mess. Sarah’s name was listed beside mine, both vying for the Senior Analyst position. Every time I glanced at the shared drive where the promotion criteria were posted, my stomach performed a tight, nauseating flip. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, and focused intensely on the swirling brown liquid, willing the tremor in my hands to subside. My throat felt suddenly dry, like I’d swallowed sand. I kept catching myself staring at the clock, the second hand ticking with agonizing slowness, each tick a countdown to some inevitable judgment.

[story 2]

"Are you sure about this, Mark?" Sarah’s voice was light, almost breezy, as she leaned over the desk, a confident smile playing on her lips. I managed a tight nod, my jaw feeling strangely locked. I cleared my throat, the sound scraping against the sudden silence in the cubicle. *Don't look weak,* I thought fiercely, forcing my gaze to meet hers. I fiddled with the edge of my keyboard, my knuckles turning white against the plastic. The air in the small space felt thick, heavy, pressing down on my chest. I could feel a rapid drumming beneath my ribs, a frantic rhythm that had nothing to do with the quarterly reports and everything to do with the precariousness of the moment.

[story 3]

I spent the entire morning meticulously proofreading my proposal, highlighting every single sentence three times, searching for some hidden flaw, some glaring oversight that would disqualify me. When Sarah finally approached me by the water cooler, her tone was warm and encouraging, which only amplified the internal churning I was experiencing. "It looks fantastic, Alex," she said, offering a genuine compliment. I managed a smile that felt brittle, a practiced, slightly too-wide expression. I took a long, deliberate sip of water, trying to steady my breathing. My gaze darted around the open-plan office, cataloging every passing face, every whispered conversation, cataloging the perceived advantages and disadvantages of my own presentation. A strange tightness constricted my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath.
